pages.text: Uniﬁed stress–strain model of concrete for FRP-conﬁned columns You-Yi Wei, Yu-Fei Wu ⇑ Department of Building and Construction, City University of Hong Kong, Tat Chee Ave., Hong Kong article info Article history: Received 13 August 2010 Received in revised form 4 June 2011 Accepted 18 June 2011 Available online 14 July 2011 Keywords: Compressive strength Concrete FRP Modeling Stress–strain model abstract This paper presents a uniﬁed stress–strain model of concrete for circular, square, and rectangular col- umns conﬁned by ﬁber-reinforced polymer (FRP) jackets. Through the uniﬁcation, the variation of param- eters is continuous and consistent, which overcomes the inconsistency or discontinuity in the results predicted by all existing models. The uniﬁcation also improves the performance of the stress–strain model by greatly extending the range of the parameter space, thus allowing a clearer picture of the trends and variations in the results. The new model is developed based on a large and up-to-date database that includes data from both the authors’ own tests and that collected from the literature, involving circular, square, and rectangular short columns and carbon FRP, glass FRP, and aramid FRP jackets with a wide range of parameter values. The proposed stress–strain model is compared with existing models, showing good agreement with the experimental results and improved performance. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Investigations in recent years have clearly demonstrated that conﬁnement with a ﬁber-reinforced polymer (FRP) jacket leads to a substantial improvement in the strength and ductility of concrete material. Numerous stress–strain models have been proposed for FRP- and steel-conﬁned concrete over the past two decades. The early stress–strain models for FRP-conﬁned concrete [1–4] evolved from models for steel-conﬁned concrete such as those proposed by Richart et al. [5,6], Ahmad and Shah [7,8], and Mander et al. [9]. Researchers later realized that FRP conﬁnement differs from steel conﬁnement, and bilinear stress–strain curves were subsequently developed for FRP-conﬁned concrete [10–15]. Several researchers, including Wu et al. [16] and Chaallal et al. [17], proposed tri-linear models to allow greater ﬂexibility and accuracy. There are also numerous nonlinear models, in which at least the ﬁrst portion (i.e., the ascending part) of the stress–strain relationship is curved. Samaan et al. [18] used the four-parameter relationship proposed by Richard and Abbott [19] to describe the stress–strain response of FRP-conﬁned concrete. The advantage of the four- parameter model is that it allows a bilinear stress–strain curve to be characterized using a single expression. Thereafter, a number of similar FRP-conﬁned concrete models appeared in the literature [20–26]. Miyauchi et al. [27,28] used Hognestad’s [29] parabola to simulate the ﬁrst portion of a stress–strain curve for FRP-conﬁned concrete, and a straight line to describe the second portion. Tout- anji [30] and Saaﬁ et al. [31] suggested a stress–strain curve using two segments of a parabola with a smooth transition. Similar two- part models have been proposed by Lillistone and Jolly [32], Li et al. [33], Jin et al. [34], Lam and Teng [35,36], Harajli et al. [37],W u et al. [38], Youssef et al. [39], and Teng et al. [40]. The difﬁculty in such models lies in the determination of the ultimate stress and corresponding strain. To date, no existing model is

The model parameters were determined and evaluated separately for different types of circular and rectangular columns on the basis of regression analysis of the experimental data. This model can be expressed as fc ¼ Ec ec 1 /C0 1 n 1 /C0 E2 Ec /C16/C17 ec eo /C16/C17 n/C0 1/C20/C21 0 6 ec 6 eo; E2 > 0 fc ¼ Ec ec 1 /C0 1 n ec eo /C16/C17 n/C0 1 /C20/C21 0 6 ec 6 eo; E2 < 0 fc ¼ fo þ E2ðec /C0 eoÞ eo 6 ec 6 ecu 8 >> >>> < >> >>> : ; ð7Þ where E2 is the slope of the second portion, and n ¼ ðEc /C0 E2Þeo Ec eo /C0 fo for E2 > 0; ð8Þ n ¼ Ec eo Ec eo /C0 fo for E 2 < 0: ð9Þ 3. The proposed model A review of the aforementioned models suggests that none can accommodate both circular and square/rectangular columns in a uniﬁed manner. For example, either different formulations or dif- ferent coefﬁcient values must be adopted for different column shapes, which does not allow a smooth transition from circular to square/rectangular columns (in the models developed by Teng’s group [35,36,40], Harajli et al. [37], or Youssef et al. [39]) or from an ascending to a descending response (in those developed by Wu et al. [38]). Furthermore, the performance of the strain-soften- ing response models is relatively poor. Finally, and more impor- tantly, a reasonably accurate model for prediction of the ultimate strain remains lacking (see Section 3.6). The aim of the work pre- sented herein was to overcome the problems inherent in earlier models or at least to make improvements. 3.1. Mathematical model Different mathematical forms for describing the stress–strain behavior of FRP-conﬁned concrete have been proposed in the liter- ature, including the one-expression nonlinear form, the two-part form with different nonlinear and linear combinations, and the tri-linear form. Two of the most frequently used expressions in the modeling of FRP-conﬁned concrete are Richard and Abbott’s four-parameter curve [19] and Hognestad’s parabola [29]. The main problem with the one-expression form is that it does not suit all types of stress–strain response curves. The drawback of Hognes- tad’s parabola is that it is unable to connect to the second portion smoothly (discontinuous slope at the intersection). To address these issues, Lam and Teng [35,36] employed a new parabola, whereas Youssef et al. [39] used a polynomial curve in the ﬁrst por- tion. Most models adopt a straight line to simulate the curve in the second portion, except those developed by Toutanji [30], Saaﬁ et al. [31], and Harajli et al. [37]. After thorough examination of different combinations of the two-part forms, the authors decided to adopt a mathematical mod- el comprising a parabola in the ﬁrst portion and a straight line in the second for the two following reasons. (1) Such a model is sim- ple yet sufﬁciently accurate for stress–strain curves with an ascending second portion; and (2) it was observed in the authors’ own tests that the variation in the shape of curves with a descend- ing second portion is complicated and sometimes inconsistent even in specimens of the same design. The use of a nonlinear form in the second portion may not increase the model’s accuracy, it sig- niﬁcantly increases its complexity. Hence, the use of a straight line for the second portion is currently considered to be a simple yet effective way of modeling FRP-conﬁned concrete. The proposed mathematical form is given by Eqs. (1

x ¼ P jExpe: /C0 Theo:jP jExpe:j ; ð13Þ where Theo. and Expe. are theoretical and experimental results, respectively. 3.4. Deﬁnition of the ultimate point Determining the ultimate point ( fcu, ecu) is no easy task. Although the deﬁnition of the ultimate point on a stress–strain curve is clear, that is, the point of FRP rupture, such rupture may not occur at a single point on the response curve. Instead, it may be a process, as shown in Fig. 3, in which the noticeable rupturing of the FRP is initiated at point A of the curve, and the ﬁnal rupture (explosion) then occurs at point B. In this work, the ﬁrst signiﬁcant rupture of the FRP (point A) is deﬁned as the ultimate point. This deﬁnition is based on the assumption that the rupturing process after the rupture at point A is unstable (signiﬁed by the wobbling of the curve) and varies signiﬁcantly from specimen to specimen even when all testing conditions are identical, whereas the starting point of the rupture (or the end point of a steady increase in conﬁnement, as signiﬁed by a smooth curve) is relatively stable. It is thus rational to deﬁne point A as the ultimate point of the stress–strain curve. This deﬁnition renders the ultimate failure points that have been collected more consistent, and leads to the better performance of the models for ultimate stress fcu and strain ecu. 3.5. Ultimate stress The ultimate stress of FRP-conﬁned concrete, fcu, is the com- pressive strength of the conﬁned concrete, fcc, when the stress– strain curve is monotonically ascending. Numerous models have been proposed to predict the conﬁned concrete strength of circular and square columns. Based on the test results shown in Fig. 4a, the second author of this paper developed a uniﬁed strength model that contains a shape factor as a function of the corner radius ratio, q =2 r/b [58], as given by the following equation: fcc fco ¼ 1 þ aqb fl fco /C18/C19 c ; ð14Þ where a, b, and c are coefﬁcients obtained by regressing the equa- tion with a particular database. Values of 2.2, 0.72, and 0.94, respec- tively, are recommended by Wu and Wang [58]. When q = 0, Eq. (14) applies to square columns with sharp corners, and when q = 1, it applies to circular columns. This model thus represents the uniﬁcation of the strength models for square (with an arbitrary corner radius) and circular columns. Based on the further test re- sults shown in Fig. 4b, the authors extended the model to rectangu- lar columns [59] by including the cross-sectional aspect ratio h/b as another parameter. This extension takes the following form: fcc fco ¼ 1 þ 2:2 2r b /C18/C19 0:72 fl fco /C18/C19 0:94 h b /C18/C19 /C0 1:9 : ð15Þ It should be noted that Eq. (15) refers to peak strength fcc, which is different from fcu for stress–strain responses with a descending branch after the peak strength, and hence a new model is required for fcu. Following the same logic and the process presented in the authors’ previous work [58,59] (also see Section 3.6), the ultimate stress can be assumed to take the following form: fcu fco ¼ a þ b 2r b /C18/C19 g fl fco /C18/C19 s h b /C18/C19 c ; ð16Þ in which the conﬁning pressure, fl, is calculated by fl ¼ 2f frpt b ¼ 2Ef efut b ð17Þ where b is the width of the column, and is equal to the diameter of circular columns or the length of the smaller side of rectangular columns. former displays greater conﬁnement efﬁciency. However, careful analyses of test data show that FRP rigidity Ef/C3 t does not have a clear and signiﬁcant effect on the ultimate strain, and hence, is not considered as a factor. Based on a consideration of these factors, four variables that may affect the ultimate strain are identiﬁed, and, accordingly, the ultimate strain can be represented by the following mathematical form: ecu eco ¼ k þ / fl fco ; 2r b ; h b ; f30 fco /C18/C19 ð19Þ where f30 is the concrete strength of unconﬁned grade C30 concrete. All of the variables in Eq. (19) are non-dimensionalized. k is a con- stant that relates the ultimate strain to the peak strain for uncon- ﬁned concrete; a value of k = 1.75 is recommended by Eurocode 2 and Ref. [40]. For the second term in Eq. (19), the trend caused by the varia- tion in the two individual factors, fl fco ; f30 fco , is relatively clear: the ultimate strain increases when the conﬁnement increases, and de- creases when the concrete strength increases. These two trends are physically well understood in the literature, and they can also be clearly seen from the variation in the results in our database. When fl is equal to zero, or fco is equal to inﬁnity, the conﬁnement effec- tiveness is zero. Hence, the effects of these two terms in Eq. (19) can be adequately described by two functions, fl fco /C16/C17 b ; f30 fco /C16/C17 k , sepa- rately. In addition, the effects of these factors work together to de- liver a combined overall effect, i.e., the overall effect increases (or decreases) when all of the individual effects increase (or decrease), and the overall effect is zero when any one of the effects is zero. These factors are thus multipliable or, mathematically, ecu eco ¼ 1:75 þ a fl fco /C18/C19 b f30 fco /C18/C19 c f 2r b /C18/C19 f h b /C18/C19 ; ð20Þ in which, the value of eco is calculated by the following equation proposed by Popovics [57] unless a test value is available. eco ¼ 0:000937 ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ fco 4 p ð21Þ For circular columns, f 2r b /C0/C1 ¼ 1 and f h b /C0/C1 ¼ 1. Regression analysis employing all of the available circular columns shows coefﬁcients a, b, and c to be 12, 0.75 and 0.62, respectively. For square columns, substituting f h b /C0/C1 ¼ 1 into Eq. ecu eco ¼ 1:75 þ 12 fl fco /C18/C19 0:75 f30 fco /C18/C19 0:62 0:36 2r b þ 0:64 /C18/C19 h b /C18/C19 /C0 0:3 : ð25Þ The ultimate strain predictions obtained with the proposed model and the other four models [36–39] are compared in Fig. 7 . It can be seen from this ﬁgure that although the proposed model has the smallest error index ( x = 0.24) of any of the models, the performance of all of the models is relatively poor when compared with the ultimate strength results shown in Fig. 5 . It is noted that test data by Al-salloum [76] and Chaallal et al. [17] are removed from the database, for that the strain of unconﬁned column are unreasonably small and the unusual stress–strain curves. Repeated efforts have been made in this work to reduce the scattering of the results and improve the performance of Eq. (25), including replacing Eq. (23) with a nonlinear function (the rela- tionship appears nonlinear in Fig. 6a). Signiﬁcant improvement ap- pears unlikely, however, because the strain results themselves have a relatively larger degree of scatter compared with the strength results, even among the three specimens in the same group with identical design in every aspect, as illustrated in Fig. 7f. Nevertheless, the proposed model does represent an improve- ment over the other models considered, and its distribution of er- ror is also relatively uniform. The model proposed by Harajli et al. is the most conservative for all types of columns. Other models generally results in unconservative prediction for circular columns. The model developed by Lam and Teng shows a relatively good dis- tribution for most square and rectangular columns, whereas that developed by Wu et al. produces an unconservative prediction for noncircular columns. 3.7. Transitional stress By best ﬁtting Eq. (10) to an experimental stress–strain curve, the transitional stress, f o, can be determined for all available test curves; that is, 103 curves for circular columns, 106 for square col- umns, and 43 for rectangular columns. The results were then sub- sequently used in regression analysis from which the following equation is obtained. fo ¼ fco þ 0:43 2r b /C18/C19 0:68 h b /C18/C19 /C0 1 fl ð26Þ Fig. 8 illustrates the performance of Eq. (26) compared with the test results, which gives a correlation factor of R2 = 0.86. 4. Performance of the proposed model Figs. 9 and 10 present a performance comparison between all of the models (the proposed model and [36–39]) and the experimen- tal results for circular and square/rectangular columns, respec- tively. All of the models produce good predictions for circular columns, apart from that proposed by Harajli et al. [37], which re- sults in conservative values for the ultimate strain, and hence over- estimates the stress in the second portion of the stress–strain curve. For square and rectangular columns, the performance of the pro- posed model and that by Lam and Teng [36] is better compared with others for strain-hardening responses, whilst the proposed model is relatively more accurate for strain softening responses. The models developed by Wu et al. [38] and Youssef et al. [39] also give a relatively good performance with some conservative for most of the specimens. Due to its poor prediction of the ultimate strain, the model proposed by Harajli et al. [37] exhibits relatively poor performance for any specimens other than their own.
